1: šimrû mišpāṭ waʿăśû ṣĕdāqâ — "keep justice and do righteousness" — the ethical imperative that opens the chapter and frames the entire inclusion oracle: the welcome of foreigners and eunuchs is grounded in covenant obedience, not merely theological sentiment. kî qĕrôbâ yĕšûʿātî lābôʾ wĕṣidqātî lĕhiggālôt — "for my salvation (yĕšûʿâ) is near to come, and my righteousness (ṣĕdāqâ) to be revealed" — the eschatological imminence is the motivation for present ethical action. The pairing of yĕšûʿâ (salvation) and ṣĕdāqâ (righteousness) — both expressing YHWH's character and act — is characteristic of Isaiah's theology of the coming restoration.
2: ʾašrê ʾĕnôš yaʿăśeh zōʾt — "blessed is the man who does this" — the beatitude structure (cf. Psalms 1:1; Isaiah 41:1; Matthew 5:3-11) grounds the blessing in action, not status. ûben-ʾādām yĕḥăzeq bāh — "and the son of man who holds fast to it" (ḥāzaq, to be strong, to grasp firmly). šōmēr šabbāt mēḥallĕlô wĕšōmēr yādô mēʿăśôt kol-rāʿ — "keeping the Sabbath without profaning it, and keeping his hand from doing any evil" — the Sabbath-keeping (šāmar šabbāt) is the specific marker of covenant participation for the expanded community of Isaiah 56:3-8. The Sabbath is the weekly enacted covenant sign.
3: wĕʾal-yōʾmar ben-hannēkār hannilweh ʾel-Yhwh lēʾmōr habdēl yabdîlēnî Yhwh mēʿal ʿammô — "let not the foreigner (ben-hannēkār, son of the foreign land) who has joined himself to YHWH say, 'YHWH will surely separate me from his people.'" The fear being addressed is the legal exclusion of Deuteronomy 23:3-8 (Ammonites, Moabites, and others excluded from the assembly). wĕʾal-yōʾmar hassārîs hēn ʾănî ʿēṣ yābēš — "and let not the eunuch say, 'Behold, I am a dry tree.'" The eunuch (sārîs, H5631) is excluded by Deuteronomy 23:1; the "dry tree" metaphor expresses his condition of having no descendants and therefore no memorial.
4: kî-kōh ʾāmar Yhwh lassārîsîm ʾăšer yišmĕrû ʾet-šabbĕtôtay — "for thus says YHWH to the eunuchs who keep my Sabbaths" — the response to the eunuch's fear is direct divine address. ûbāḥărû baʾăšer ḥāpāṣtî ûmaḥăzîqîm bibĕrîtî — "who choose what pleases me and hold fast my covenant" — the three criteria (Sabbath-keeping, choosing what pleases YHWH, holding fast the covenant) define the community membership in terms of character and allegiance rather than birth status. The inclusion is based on obedience, not on reversing the physical condition.
5: wĕnātattî lāhem bĕbêtî ûbĕḥômōtay yāḏ wāšēm — "I will give them in my house and within my walls a monument and a name (yāḏ wāšēm)" — yāḏ (hand/memorial stele) and šēm (name) together form the idea of a permanent public monument — more enduring than biological descendants. ṭôḇ mibbānîm ûmibbānôt — "better than sons and daughters" — the reversal is complete: what the eunuch lacked (children as memorial) is exceeded by what YHWH gives. šēm ʿôlām ʾettēn-lô ʾăšer lōʾ yikkārēt — "an everlasting name I will give him that shall not be cut off" — the permanent name (šēm ʿôlām) is the ultimate reversal of the eunuch's fear of namelessness.
6: ûbĕnê hannēkār hannilwîm ʿal-Yhwh lĕšārĕtô — "and the foreigners who join themselves to YHWH, to serve him" — lāwâ (to join oneself to, to attach to) is the same root as "Levi" — the joining of foreigners to YHWH mirrors the tribal name that meant "attached." ûlĕʾahăbâ ʾet-šēm Yhwh lihyôt lô laʿăḇādîm — "to love the name of YHWH, and to be his servants." kol-šōmēr šabbāt mēḥallĕlô ûmaḥăzîqîm bibĕrîtî — "everyone who keeps the Sabbath without profaning it and holds fast my covenant" — the Sabbath is again the covenant marker for this extended community.
7: wahăḇîʾôtîm ʾel-har qoḏšî wĕśimmahttîm bĕbêt tĕpillātî — "I will bring them to my holy mountain and make them joyful in my house of prayer" — the phrase bêt tĕpillātî (my house of prayer) is the crux. ʿôlōtêhem wĕzibḥêhem lĕrāṣôn ʿal-mizbĕḥî — "their burnt offerings and sacrifices will be accepted on my altar." kî bêtî bêt-tĕpillâ yiqqārēʾ lĕkol-hāʿammîm — "for my house shall be called a house of prayer for all peoples (kol-hāʿammîm)" — the universalist climax. Jesus quotes this phrase in Mark 11:17; Matthew 21:13; Luke 19:46 when cleansing the temple, indicting the traders who have turned the house of all-peoples prayer into a commercial operation, and specifically a den of robbers (mĕʿarat pārīṣîm) drawn from Jeremiah 7:11.
8: nĕʾum ʾădōnāy Yhwh mĕqabbēṣ nidḥê yiśrāʾēl — "oracle of the Lord YHWH, who gathers the outcasts (nidḥê, scattered/banished ones) of Israel" — the ingathering is already underway for Israel. ʿôd ʾăqabbēṣ ʿālāyw lĕniqbāṣāyw — "I will gather still more to him besides those already gathered" — the gathering extends beyond Israel to the foreigners and eunuchs of Isaiah 56:3-7. The word ʿôd (still/again) signals that the community expansion is not finished; YHWH will continue adding to his people.
9: kol-ḥayyat haśśādeh ʾĕtāyû lĕʾākōl kol-ḥayyat bayyāʿar — "all you beasts of the field, come to eat; all you beasts in the forest." The sudden shift from the inclusion oracle (Isaiah 56:1-8) to a summons to wild animals is jarring. The animals are summoned to consume something — what? The context (Isaiah 56:10-12) identifies the corrupt leaders as the target: the watchmen who fail, the shepherds who only think about their own appetite, are abandoned to become the prey of wild beasts.
10: ṣōpāyw ʿiwwĕrîm kullām — "his watchmen (ṣōpeh, H6822, lookout/sentinel) are all blind" — the failure of Israel's spiritual leadership. lōʾ yāḏĕʿû kullām — "they have no knowledge at all." kullām kĕlāḇîm ʾillĕmîm lōʾ yûklû linbōaḥ — "they are all dumb dogs that cannot bark" — the watchdog who cannot bark is the most specific failure: the guard animal's one function is warning, and it fails to give warning. The blind watchman and the silent watchdog are images of pastoral and prophetic failure. hōzîm šōkĕḇîm ʾōhăḇê lānûm — "dreaming, lying down, loving to slumber."
11: wĕhakkĕlāḇîm ʿazzê-nepeš lōʾ yāḏĕʿû śobʿâ — "the dogs have a mighty appetite (ʿazzê-nepeš, strong of soul/appetite) and are never satisfied" — the insatiable desire is the defining character of the failed leadership. wĕhēmmâ rōʿîm lōʾ yāḏĕʿû hāḇîn — "and they are shepherds who have no understanding." The word rōʿeh (shepherd, leader/pastor) connects to the Good Shepherd imagery of Isaiah 40:11: the shepherd who gathers lambs contrasted with these shepherds who think only of their own appetite. kullām lĕdarkām pānû ʾîš lĕbiṣʿô miqṣēhû — "they have all turned to their own way, each one to his own gain (beṣāʿ, unjust gain, plunder)."
12: lĕkû ʾeqqĕḥâ-yāyin wĕnisĕbĕʾâ šēkār — "Come, let me get wine and let us drink strong drink" — the self-invitation of the corrupt leaders to continued indulgence. wĕhāyâ kāzeh māḥār yôm gādôl yōtēr mĕʾōd — "and tomorrow will be like this day, even much more so" — the presumptuous confidence in continuation (cf. Luke 12:19-20's rich fool: "Soul, you have ample goods laid up for many years"). The chapter ends with this damning characterization of Israel's leadership: blind watchmen, insatiable dogs, presumptuous drinkers — the antithesis of the obedient, covenant-keeping foreigners and eunuchs of Isaiah 56:1-8 who receive YHWH's welcome.
